coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots synthesis via one step hydrothermal carbonization of green alga and their multifunctional applications
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuehai
Liu, Fengjiao
Cai, Jiabai
Huang, Xuguang
Lin, Luxiu
Lin, Ye
Yang, Hui
Li, Shunxing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N/S-CDs) were prepared with the model bloom-forming green alga Dunaliella salina via a one-step hydrothermal reaction, i.e., no chemicals were required. The obtained N/S-CDs with average particle size about 3.2 ± 0.5 nm exhibited great photostability, low toxicity, high water solubility, excitation-dependent emission, and enough fluorescent activity. According to the synergy effect of the co-doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms, N/S-CDs were used as multifunctional fluorescent probes for algal imaging, scavenging of 1, 1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl and hydroxyl radicals, selective detection of Fe(III), and visible light-induced photodegradation of methylene blue and methyl violet. In a word, harmful green alga blooms could be transformed into multifunctional N/S-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doping
Multifunctional carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Photocatalyst
Antioxidant
Green alga

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell viability assay and algal imaging
Optical properties and stability of N/S-CDs
Cell viability assay and fluorescence imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N/S-CDs. (b) Size distribution histogram of N/S-CDs. (c) AFM image, and (d) the corresponding height image of N/S-CDs.
(a) FTIR and (b) XPS survey spectra of N/S-CDs.
(a) C1s, (b) O1s, (c) N1s and (d) S2p core-level spectra of the N/S-CDs.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation (Ex = 322 nm), and fluorescence emission (Em = 410 nm) of the N/S-CDs solution (b) Fluorescence spectra of the N/S-CDs solution at different fluorescence excitation wavelengths.

(a) The cell viability percentage of Thalassiosira weisslflogii and Dunaliella salina cells cultured with different concentrations of N/S-CDs (0, 100, 200, 400 μg·mL−1). (b) The confocal fluorescence image of Dunaliella salina cultured with 100 μg·mL−1 of N/S-CDs for 48 h.

(a) Scavenging of DPPH radicals (100 μmol·L−1) and hydroxyl radicals with different concentrations of N/S-CDs. (b) Clearance of DPPH radicals and (c) hydroxyl radicals with different concentrations of ascorbic acid.
Photodegradation rates of (a) methylene blue and (b) methyl violet using N/S-CDs and CDs under the same conditions.
FTIR spectrum of commercial undoped carbon dots.

(a) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of various metal ions (0.5 mmol·L−1) added to the N/S-CDs in the absence (blue bar) and in the presence (4 mmol·L−1, red bar) of EDTA. (b) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of in the absence (blue bar) and in the presence (0.5 mmol·L−1, red bar) of Fe(III) in the N/S-CDs solution including corresponding metal ions (0.5 mmol·L−1) and EDTA (4 mmol·L−1).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra (Em = 412 nm) of N/S-CDs with different concentrations of Fe(III). (b) Linear relationship of (F0 − F)/F0 versus Fe(III) concentration ranged from 1 to 100 μmol·L−1. F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs in the EDTA (4 mmol·L−1) solution without and with different concentrations of Fe(III), respectively.

(a) Stern-Volmer curves between F0/F and Fe(III) concentration at 25 °C and 45 °C. F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs in EDTA solution without and with different concentrations of Fe(III). (b) The change of absorption spectrum of N/S-CDs with Fe(III).

Fluorescence decay curves of N/S-CDs without (a) and with (b) Fe (III) addition (final concentration 25 μmol·L−1, Ex = 322 nm). Surface potential distribution of N/S-CDs without (c) and with (d) Fe(III) addition.
Comparison of the ability of different CDs to scavenge free radicals.

row.entry: And the corresponding concentration of CDs (μg·mL−1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of fluorescence nanomaterial based method for detection Fe(III).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: N-CDs = Nitrogen doped carbon dots; g-CNQDs = g-C3N4 quantum dots; N/P-CDs = Nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe(III) in actual water samples by N/S-CDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots synthesis via one step hydrothermal carbonization of green alga and their multifunctional applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Pollution Monitoring and Control & Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Pollution Monitoring and Control & Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N/S-CDs) were prepared with the model bloom-forming green alga Dunaliella salina via a one-step hydrothermal reaction, i.e., no chemicals were required. The obtained N/S-CDs with average particle size about 3.2 ± 0.5 nm exhibited great photostability, low toxicity, high water solubility, excitation-dependent emission, and enough fluorescent activity. According to the synergy effect of the co-doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms, N/S-CDs were used as multifunctional fluorescent probes for algal imaging, scavenging of 1, 1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl and hydroxyl radicals, selective detection of Fe(III), and visible light-induced photodegradation of methylene blue and methyl violet. In a word, harmful green alga blooms could be transformed into multifunctional N/S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widely attention in sensing [1,2], bioimaging [3], drug delivery [4], pHotodynamic therapy [5], photocatalysis [6] and electrocatalysis [7] because of their good photoluminescence, excellent photostability, low cytotoxicity, highly efficient electron-accepting capabilities, great biocompatibility, and resistance to metabolic degradation in bioapplications. In addition, multiple dopants in CDs can be more suitable for sensor and catalytic applications [8–12]. Because the electron-rich nitrogen atoms in CDs contribute additional electrons to the conduction band, the thermal oxidation stability and electrical conductivity are improved [13,14]. Further, nitrogen doped CDs have been exploited available functional groups for target fluorescence sensing [15] and heavy metal adsorb [16]. But the study of sulfur-doped CDs is limited. As we know there was only one study about sulfur-doped CDs as selective fluorescent probe for Fe(III) detection, because sulfur not only could tune the electronic density but also enhance the coordination between Fe(III) and hydroxyl groups in the sulfur doped CDs surface [17]. Compared to mono-nitrogen or sulfur doping, the synergistic effect of sulfur and nitrogen co-doping on the properties of CDs is obvious, including longer fluorescence emission wavelength and higher quantum yield [18,19], more excellent electrocatalytic and photocatalytic activity [20–23]. However, the preparation methods of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N/S-CDs) are usually complex, generally involving extreme conditions and extra purification (or passivation) processes [19,21,24,25]. So a simple, convenient, and green method to obtain N/S-CDs need to be explored.

Sulfur-containing amino acids as the carbon, nitrogen and sulfur source are commonly found in bloom-forming green alga [26], which are present in almost all environments ranging from seawater to fresh water and hot springs. Based on bloom-forming and widespread green alga, harmful algal blooms could be transformed into multifunctional N/S-CDs by a green and economic method (e.g., one step hydrothermal reaction) without any chemicals needed. Dunaliella salina was selected as a model alga for its sulfur-containing amino acids, including methionine (about 0.15%) and cysteine (about 0.032%) [27].

The early functions of CDs were focused on imaging and sensors [1,2,24,25]. In recent years, CDs begin to be used for antioxidants and sensors [20,26]. However, multi-functional CDs simultaneously as antioxidants, sensors, bioimaging agents, and photocatalyst have not been reported. Therefore, the properties of N/S-CDs derived from green alga Dunaliella salina were tested, including the photostability in the solution with different temperature, pH and ionic strength, photobleaching, fluorescence quantum yield, surface charge property, and toxicity. Further, the potential multi-functional applications of the prepared N/S-CDs in the fields of bioimaging, fluorescent sensor, biomedicine (antioxidant), and photocatalysis were explored. The experimental results show the harmful green alga blooms could be transformed into multifunctional N/S-CDs.

Commercial un-doped CDs were purchased from Beida Jubang Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 1, 1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and quinine sulfate were bought from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), Trypsin and DNAse I were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Methylene blue trihydrate, methyl violet, alcohol, ascorbic acid, Na2EDTA, Mg(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, FeCl2, Fe(NO3)3, MnCl2, Cu(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, CrCl3, CdCl3, Pb(NO3)2, and HgCl2 were obtained from west long chemical Co., Ltd. (Shantou, China), AgNO3 was obtained from Shanghai shenbo chemical, Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Without further purification, all analytical grade chemicals were used directly. As a solvent, the deionized water was further purified by a Millipore-Q system (Millipore Co., USA) until its resistivity was exceeded 18.2 MΩ·cm−1.

A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan), Multimode 8 system (Bruker, Germany), Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Nicolet iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), FLS 920 fluorescence spectrometer with a time correlation of single photon counting system (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), Zetasizer Nano ZS90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK), Flowsight Quantitative Imaging Analysis Flow Cytometry (ImageStreamXMark Type II, amnis), UV-5800PC UV–visible spectrophotometer (METASH, China), Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Malaysia), TCS SP8 laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica, Germany), and 7500cx ICP-MS (Agilent Technologies, USA) were used for the measurements of transmission electron microscope (TEM), X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and atomic force microscope (AFM) images, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), time-resolved FL spectra, surface zeta potential, in vitro cytotoxicity experiments, UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, confocal laser scanning microscopy images, and metal ion concentration.

Green alga Dunaliella salina got from the State Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Science, Xiamen University, China. It was cultured by some steps which indicated in the supporting information. And one step hydrothermal method was developed for N/S-CDs synthesis as follows. After centrifugation of Dunaliella salina with a speed of 7800 rpm for 4 min, washing three times with water, and drying completely in a vacuum oven at 60 °C, the collected green alga was grinded to uniform powder. After ultrasound dispersion of 0.2 g algal powder in a Teflon-sealed autoclave with 50 mL of H2O, Dunaliella salina was heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The resultant was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane for removing large particles and the filtrate was further purified by dialysis in a dialysis bag (MWCO 100 Da) for 24 h. The filtrate was lyophilized as a solid sample and re-dissolved in water, according to the required concentration.

According to the fluorescence (FL) excitation wavelength (Ex = 322 nm) of N/S-CDs, quinine sulfate was adopted as the reference for its similar excitation wavelength (350 nm) and high quantum yield (54.6%) [28]. The N/S-CDs (u) and quinine sulfate (s) were dissolved in the water and H2SO4 solution (0.1 mol·L−1), respectively, which absorbance was maintained below 0.05 at 322 nm. The quantum yield (Y) of the prepared N/S-CDs (Yu) was measured and calculated according to the formula [29].
Y u
=
Y s
F u
/
F s
A s
/
A u
Here “F” and “A” are the integral emission intensity and the UV–visible absorbance at the excitation wavelength, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay and algal imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of N/S-CQDs was tested using Thalassiosira weisslflogii (TW) and Dunaliella salina (DS) cells to evaluate the biocompatibility and fluorescence imaging feasibility of N/S-CQDs. The phytoplankton was cultured in f/2 medium with different concentrations of N/S-CDs (0, 100, 200, 400 μg mL−1). The algal cell viability was measured after one growth cycle (10 days). 1 mL of TW and DS cells medium were collected in duplicate. One was directly counted by flow cytometry for the total number of cells, another was counted for the number of viable cells. The viable cells were prepared as follows: the collected medium was added with 200 uL DNAse I solution and heated in 37 °C by water bath for 15 min, then continued adding 200 μL Trypsin solution and kept 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, the cells were placed on ice (about 5 min) to terminate the enzyme reaction, and counted by flow cytometry immediately. Three experiments were performed in parallel. Cell viability was calculated by the following equation:
Cell viability
%
= the number of viable cells / total number of cells

N/S-CQDs were used for algal cell fluorescence imaging. First, Dunaliella salina was cultured in f/2 medium that contained 100 μg mL−1 of N/S-CDs. After 48 h the cells were centrifuged and washed three times with phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) and once with the ultrapure water. Finally, the algal cells were fixed in paraformaldehyde solution and observed by the laser scanning confocal microscopy.

The antioxidant activity of N/S-CDs was tested by scavenge DPPH and hydroxyl radicals. The DPPH radical has a single electron and its ethanol solution is deep purple for its characteristic absorbance peak at 517 nm. This solution could be faded by the pair with a free radical scavenger and the fading degree is in quantitative relationship with the amount of electrons accepted by the DPPH radical. Therefore, a rapid quantitative analysis can be performed using the UV–visible spectrophotometer [30]. In the experiment, 10 μL of N/S-CDs solutions with different concentration were added into DPPH ethanol solution (3 mL, 100 μmol·L−1), respectively, placed in the dark for 30 min, and then the UV–visible absorbance of the DPPH free radicals in the absence and presence of N/S-CDs (i.e., Ac and As) was measured at 517 nm, respectively. Using the following equation, the scavenging DPPH radical activity was estimated.
Scavenging
%
=
A c
−
A s
/
A c
× 100 %

Salicylic acid could be transformed by hydroxyl radicals into purple 2, 3-dihydroxybenzoic acid, which has a strong absorption at 510 nm, but the transformation reaction could be weakened by the addition of a hydroxyl radical scavenger. In the experiment, hydroxyl radicals were produced by the Fenton reaction. Salicylic acid (ethanol solution, 1.5 mL, 1.8 mmol·L−1), N/S-CDs (1 mL, 0–500 μg·mL−1), and H2O2 (0.1 mL, 100 mmol·L−1) were added successively into FeSO4 solution (2 mL, 1.8 mmol·L−1) at 37 °C. After reaction for 30 min, the UV–visible absorbance (A0 and As for the absence and presence of N/S-CDs, respectively) was measured at 510 nm and then the scavenging rate of hydroxyl radicals by N/S-CDs was calculated as follow.
Scavenging
%
=
A 0
−
A s
/
A 0
× 100 %

Both methylene blue (MB) and methyl violet (MV) were selected as the photodegradation targets for the assessment of the photocatalytic activity of N/S-CDs and commercial undoped CDs (CDs). CDs or N/S-CDs (6 mg) was dispersed in MB (10 mL, 20 mg·L−1) and MV (20 mL, 5 mg L−1) solutions, diluted into 40 mL and 30 mL, respectively, and stirred for 1 h in the dark. Afterwards, the above mixtures were irradiated under visible light, which was obtained by using filter to remove the UV light (λ < 420 nm) from a 300 W xenon lamp placed 15 cm away from the solution surface. The light intensity on the solution surface was about 318 W/m2. The UV–visible absorbance of the samples at 664 nm for MB (or 580 nm for MV) was taken before and after irradiation every 10 min for MB (or 30 min for MV).

N/S-CDs (4 μL, 26 mg·L−1), EDTA (200 μL, 100 mmol·L−1), and Fe(NO3)3 (appropriate amount) were added into the test solution at room temperature, which volume was kept at 5 mL and the standard concentrations of Fe(III) were 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500 μmol·L−1, respectively. Meanwhile, river water and seawater as actual samples from Jiu Long River and Taiwan Strait (22.65°N, 118.82°E), respectively, were filtered through 0.22 μm filters and used for Fe(III) determination and their spiked experiment. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 322 nm (excitation wavelength) under the optimal conditions.

Based on the standard reference (quinine sulfate), the fluorescence quantum yields of N/S-CDs prepared under different hydrothermal conditions, including temperature and time, are shown in Table S1. The experimental results showed that the N/S-CDs could be synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using Dunaliella salina as biomass precursor, the optimal hydrothermal temperature and time were 200 °C and 5 h, and the highest fluorescence quantum yield was 5.93%.

As shown in TEM image (Fig. 1a–b), N/S-CDs were round in shape and their diameter was concentrated at 3.2 nm. As shown in AFM scanning images (Fig. 1c–d), the height of N/S-CDs were about 3.2–4.0 nm. These results reveal that the shape and uniform size of N/S-CDs were spherical and about 3.2 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the surface functional groups of N/S-CDs were disclosed by FTIR. The wide peak of 3396 cm−1 and two peaks of 2961 and 2929 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of OH and the fine structure of intramolecular hydrogen bond formed by the surface carboxyl groups [31], respectively. The stretching vibration peaks of CO, CN, and CS bonds were observed at 1660 cm−1, 1385/1140 cm−1, and 623 cm−1, respectively [24,32–34]. In summary, the good water solubility of N/S-CDs was due to the surface hydrophilic groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

XPS further illustrated the elemental composition of the synthesized N/S-CDs. Fig. 2b shows that N/S-CDs synthesized by Dunaliella salina mainly contained five elements, including C (66.4%), O (26.5%), N (5.0%), S (1.2%), and Na (0.9%). Moreover, Dunaliella salina was cultured in seawater with f/2 medium (including NaNO3, NaH2PO4, Na2SiO3, Na2EDTA, Na2MoO4 and so on). Therefore, it has some residual Na in N/S-CDs.

The chemical bond information of the synthesized N/S-CDs was determined by the core-level spectra. Three kinds of chemical bonds, CC/CC, CS/CO, and CO, were disclosed by three peaks at 284.7, 286.1 and 287.6 eV in the C1s core-level spectra (Fig. 3a) [24]. Two peaks at 531.5 and 532.6 eV in the O1s core-level spectra (Fig. 3b) were corresponding to the two chemical bonds of CO and COH [33]. And in the N1s core-level spectra (Fig. 3c), there were two peaks at 399.7 and 401.6 eV, which were from the two chemical bonds of CNC and NH, respectively [24]. The presence of CS and CSOx was proven by the two peaks at 163.7 and 168.6 eV in the S2p core-level spectra (Fig. 3d) [35]. The presence of above four main elements in the synthesized N/S-CDs was also confirmed. Therefore, C, N, and S from sulfur-containing amino acids in the green alga could be transformed into chemical composition of the synthesized N/S-CDs via a one-step hydrothermal reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of N/S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A weak UV–vis R absorption band at 250–310 nm was observed (Fig. 4a), so N/S-CDs contained unsaturated heteroatom groups, such as carbonyl group, etc. As shown in Fig. 4a, the synthesized N/S-CDs had a fluorescence excitation peak at 322 nm (Ex = 322 nm) which was near the UV–vis absorption band and a symmetrical fluorescence emission peak at 412 nm (Em = 412 nm). Therefore, the N/S-CDs solution was transparent and light yellow under daylight but emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. 4b, even if the fluorescence excitation wavelengths increased gradually from 282 nm to 322 nm with a increment of 10 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelengths of N/S-CDs were the same, but their intensities were gradually increased, i.e., the strongest fluorescence intensity was obtained at 322 nm excitation. The continuous increase of fluorescence excitation wavelength from 322 nm to 362 nm caused a red shift of fluorescence emission wavelengths and the decrease of fluorescence intensity, i.e., excitation-dependent emission was observed, which might be due to the existence of different particle size or different surface emission defects [31].

With the increase of solution temperature from 15 °C to 30 °C, the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs decreased slightly (Fig. S1a), which can be ignored for the application requirements of environment temperature. As shown in Fig. S1b, although the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs increased with the increasing of solution pH values, it was relatively stable at pH 5–9 and was suitable for natural water samples. The effect of high ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs was not obvious and even the fluorescence intensity was keep at 88.2% in 3 mol·L−1 NaCl solution (Fig. S1c), which revealed that N/S-CDs were suitable as fluorescence probe for seawater. After exposure N/S-CDs under UV light irradiation for 200 min, the fluorescence intensity remained 96.0% (Fig. S1d), i.e., its resistant to photobleaching ability was excellent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Thalassiosira weisslflogii and Dunaliella salina cells were selected for the cytotoxicity and practicality assessment of N/S-CDs for cell imaging (Fig. 5a). After incubation with N/S-CDs (200 μg·mL−1) for one growth cycle, the viability of both cells was very close to 100%. Even incubation with high N/S-CDs concentration (400 μg·mL−1), the cell viabilities of TW and DS cells still reached 90.6% and 92.4%, respectively. However, the concentration of fluorescence probe actually used for bioimaging was only around 150 μg·mL−1 [36]. A strong fluorescence from the cytoplasm of DS cells (Fig. 5b) indicated that N/S-CDs can permeate into the cells and remain fluorescent properties in the cell environment. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of N/S-CDs can be ignored and N/S-CDs are suitable as the fluorescent probe in biological applications.

The antioxidant activity of N/S-CDs was tested with the scavenging activity of DPPH free radicals and hydroxyl radicals. After scavenged by N/S-CDs (i.e., free radical scavengers), DPPH free radicals were transformed into stable DPPH-H complex, which color was changed from deep purple to light yellow. As demonstrated in Fig. 6a, the scavenging rate of DPPH free radicals and hydroxyl radicals increased with the increase of N/S-CDs concentration and the highest scavenging rate of DPPH free radicals and hydroxyl radical was 67.3% and 71.2%, using 500 and 400 μg·mL−1 of N/S-CDs, respectively. Meanwhile, ascorbic acid (AA) was selected for positive control experiment. The scavenging rate of DPPH free radicals and hydroxyl radicals was continuously increased with the increase of ascorbic acid concentration, and the rate tended to be stable at 95.9% and 99.3% when AA was 7 and 130 μg·mL−1, respectively (Fig. 6b–c).

Based on the above analysis, the scavenging ability of N/S-CDs for DPPH free radicals and hydroxyl radical was relatively better than similar CDs [24], which maximum removal rate of DPPH radicals and hydroxyl radicals were 56% and 58%, using 500 and 800 μg·mL−1 of CDs, respectively. And the detailed comparison is in the Table 1 . The mechanism of free radical scavenging can be explained by two factors. The particle size of N/S-CDs was so small that the surface-to-volume ratio was considerable. Moreover, the surface of N/S-CD had much reducing groups, such as OH, CO, CN, CS, etc. These properties made N/S-CDs tend to react with and reduce electron-rich free radicals. [37]

Many kinds of metal ions are coexisted in natural water, including Na(I), Ca(II), Mg(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Cu(II), Zn(II), Co(II), Ni(II), Cd(III), Pb(II), Hg(II), Mn(II), Ag(I), Cr(III), and Cr(VI), so the fluorescence selective response is crucial. As shown in Fig. 9a, Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II) and Cr(VI) had relatively obvious fluorescence quenching effect on N/S-CDs than the control group (without adding metal ions). After addition of 4 mmol·L−1 (final concentration) of EDTA, only Fe(III) caused a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity. Compared with the control group (without metal ions), the fluorescence quenching effect was very weak and similar. However, after adding 0.5 mmol·L−1 (final concentration) of Fe(III) to each solution with various metal ions, the fluorescence quenching effects were obvious and very close to each other (Fig. 9b). These revealed that N/S-CDs in the EDTA-added reaction system have a strong fluorescence recognition effect on Fe(III), and the fluorescence of N/S-CDs was quenched selectively by Fe(III).

The above results may be explained as follows. As shown in Fig. 9a, the fluorescence of N/S-CDs was quenched by the addition of Fe(III), Hg(II), Cr(VI), and Cu(II) respectively. These kinds of metal ions can combine with N/S-CDs via its surface functional groups (CO, CN, CS, COH, NH, CNC, CSOx, seen in Figs. 2a and 3) and trigger the fluorescence quenching. When 4 mmol·L−1 of EDTA (carboxyl and amino group as functional groups) was added into the solutions of Cu(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Hg(II), and Cr(VI), the fluorescence was restored, i.e., N/S-CDs combined with these metals could be replaced by EDTA, which might be due to the enhancement of complex stabilities from metal-N/S-CDs to metal-EDTA. The stability constant of metal ion and EDTA complex (lg KMY) is high as 21.7, 18.8, 18.62, and 18.04 for Hg(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II), respectively. When Fe(III) was added further, the fluorescence of N/S-CDs was quenched sharply (seen in Fig. 9b), i.e., Fe(III) was combined with N/S-CDs, rather than with EDTA, due to the strong binding preference of Fe(III) toward the surface functional groups on the surface of the N/S-CDs.

Based on previous exploration, a highly selective fluorescence detection method for Fe(III) could be constructed using N/S-CDs as the fluorescent probe. The effect of Fe(III) addition (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500 μmol·L−1 in 4 mmol·L−1 of EDTA) on the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence emission peak was shown in Fig. 10a, and the higher Fe(III) concentration, the weaker corresponding fluorescence intensity. A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9985) between fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0 − F)/F0) and Fe(III) concentration (0.1–100 μmol·L−1) was obtained and shown in Fig. 10b. The limit of detection was 66.69 nmol·L−1 (S/N = 3), which was much less than the drinking water standard of Fe(III) (5.357 μmol·L−1) set by the USEPA. A comparison of fluorescence nanomaterial based method for detection Fe(III) is showed in Table 2 . It can be drawn that the fluorescence method based on N/S-CDs had relatively wide linear range and high sensitivity.

Stern-Volmer linear relationship between F0/F and Fe(III) concentration (0.1–100 μmol·L−1) was obtained at 25 °C and 45 °C with a Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) of 6.43 × 103 L·mol−1 and 6.60 × 103 L·mol−1 (Fig. 11a). These two KSV were so large and similar that we could speculate that the fluorescence quenching process was a static quenching [28]. Another side, the absorption spectrum of N/S-CDs with Fe(III) compared to spectrum of N/S-CDs was different as shown in Fig. 11b. It illustrated that N/S-CDs and Fe(III) had formatted the new complex.

In order to further confirm the static quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of N/S-CDs with or without Fe(III) addition was carried out. As shown in Fig. 12a–b, the fluorescence decay curves of N/S-CDs without and with Fe(III) addition were well fitted by the double exponential function. The fluorescence lifetimes of N/S-CDs without and with Fe(III) addition were 6.42 ns and 6.43 ns, respectively, which indicating that the electron donor elements such as N, S or negatively charged groups on the surface of N/S-CDs coupled with Fe(III) closely trigger the fluorescence static quenching. As shown in Fig. 12c, the surface potential of N/S-CDs is −23.8 mV. After added Fe(III), the surface potential of N/S-CDs was −5.46 mV (Fig. 12d), indicating that Fe(III) was coordinated with negatively charged groups on the N/S-CDs surface and then reduced the surface potential of N/S-CDs.

In order to verify the practicality of N/S-CDs as fluorescence probe for selective determination of Fe(III) in river water and seawater, standard addition-recovery experiments were performed. The results are shown in Table 3 . The recoveries of Fe(III) were in the range of 91.60–117.3% and the RSD were <5.1%. The results of this method and ICP-MS were consistent, indicating that the method based on N/S-CDs as fluorescence probe was suitable for the determination of Fe(III) in actual water samples.

To the best of our knowledge, using bloom-forming green alga as the precursor to prepare N/S-CDs via the one-step hydrothermal method is the first time. Because of the synthesized N/S-CDs with uniform particle size (about 3.2 nm), negatively charged and functionalized surface (including the surface groups of hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, CS, and CSOx), strong fluorescence stability and photobleaching resistance, wide environment adaptability (temperature 15–30 °C, pH 5–9, ionic strength 0–3 mol·L−1 NaCl), low biological toxicity, and appropriate antioxidation, N/S-CDs were suitable for algal bioimaging, fluorescence probe, and photodegradation. Based on the static quenching effect of Fe(III) on the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs, a selective fluorescence detection method for Fe(III) in actual water samples, especially for seawater with high ionic strength was constructed, with a wide linear range (0.1–100 μmol·L−1) and a low detection limit (66.69 nmol·L−1, S/N = 3). In summary, harmful green alga blooms could be transformed into multifunctional N/S-CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675077 and 21475055), the Education-Science Research Project for Young and Middle-aged Teachers of Fujian (No. JAT170341), the Natural Science Fund of Zhangzhou (No. ZZ2018J16).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.04.015.
